title: Lab: ARP Resolution and Security Analysis
description:
Analyze ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) traffic showing normal address resolution, gratuitous ARP announcements, duplicate address detection (DAD), and an ARP spoofing attack. Understand how Layer 2 addressing works and recognize security threats.

Use the PCAP viewer below to examine each ARP packet and answer the analysis questions.
hints: ARP maps IP addresses (Layer 3) to MAC addresses (Layer 2)
ARP requests are broadcast (ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff); ARP replies are unicast
ARP Probes use sender IP 0.0.0.0 to test for duplicate addresses
Gratuitous ARP announces IP-to-MAC mappings to update all ARP caches
ARP spoofing sends fake replies to poison ARP caches — a man-in-the-middle attack

labQuestions.question: Why is the ARP request in packet #1 sent to the broadcast MAC address (ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff)?
options: ARP always uses broadcast for both requests and replies
The sender doesn't know the target's MAC address yet, so it must ask all hosts on the LAN
Broadcast is required by IPv4 for all Layer 2 communication
The sender's ARP cache is corrupted
labQuestions.correctAnswer: The sender doesn't know the target's MAC address yet, so it must ask all hosts on the LAN

labQuestions.question: In packet #1, the Target MAC field is 00:00:00:00:00:00. Why?
options: It's a broadcast address
The target's MAC is unknown — that's exactly what the ARP request is trying to discover
The target host is offline
This indicates an ARP error
labQuestions.correctAnswer: The target's MAC is unknown — that's exactly what the ARP request is trying to discover

labQuestions.question: What is the gateway's MAC address as revealed in the ARP reply (packet #2)?
In packet #3, the Ethernet destination is 00:1a:2b:3c:4d:5e (gateway MAC), but the IP destination is 93.184.216.34 (remote server). Why do the Layer 2 and Layer 3 destinations differ?
options: This is an error in the packet capture
The packet must be sent to the local gateway (Layer 2 MAC) to be routed to the remote server (Layer 3 IP)
The remote server is on the same LAN as the client
Layer 2 and Layer 3 always have different destinations
labQuestions.correctAnswer: The packet must be sent to the local gateway (Layer 2 MAC) to be routed to the remote server (Layer 3 IP)

labQuestions.question: What makes packet #4 an ARP Probe (Duplicate Address Detection) rather than a normal ARP request?
options: It uses a different EtherType
The sender IP is 0.0.0.0 — the host hasn't claimed the IP yet
The target MAC is set to its own MAC
It's sent via unicast instead of broadcast
labQuestions.correctAnswer: The sender IP is 0.0.0.0 — the host hasn't claimed the IP yet

labQuestions.question: Packet #6 shows a suspicious ARP reply. What security attack does this represent?
options: DNS spoofing
ARP spoofing/poisoning — a fake reply to redirect traffic through the attacker
MAC flooding attack
DHCP starvation attack
labQuestions.correctAnswer: ARP spoofing/poisoning — a fake reply to redirect traffic through the attacker

labQuestions.question: How can you tell packet #6 is an ARP spoofing attack? Compare it to the legitimate response in packet #2.
options: The packet is larger than a normal ARP reply
It claims 192.168.1.1 is at MAC de:ad:be:ef:00:01, which differs from the real gateway MAC (00:1a:2b:3c:4d:5e)
It uses UDP instead of ARP
The TTL value is too high
labQuestions.correctAnswer: It claims 192.168.1.1 is at MAC de:ad:be:ef:00:01, which differs from the real gateway MAC (00:1a:2b:3c:4d:5e)

labQuestions.question: What Layer 2 security feature can prevent ARP spoofing attacks on managed switches?
options: Port forwarding
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)
Network Address Translation (NAT)
DNS over HTTPS (DoH)
labQuestions.hint: DAI validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table
